aliases: Thick Toast with Butter and Sugar

english: Thick Toast with Butter and Sugar

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic toast entry (FOODON_03315091). No specific entry exists for butter-sugar thick toast as a Singaporean kopitiam preparation. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers bread and similar products. No specific code exists for Singaporean thick toast with butter and sugar. Closest parent category used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for this specific Singaporean kopitiam toast preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving consists of two thick slices of toasted white bread (approximately 120 g total) spread generously with butter and sprinkled with white sugar, as served at Singaporean kopitiam establishments such as Toast Box.

nutrient_source.title: Bread, white, commercially prepared (toasted) with butter and sugar — estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for white toast bread (NDB 18069), butter (NDB 01001), and granulated sugar (NDB 19335), combined in proportions consistent with kopitiam-style thick toast preparation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Singaporean butter-sugar thick toast exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for white toast bread (~55% by weight), butter (~25% by weight), and white sugar (~20% by weight) to reflect a typical kopitiam thick toast preparation. Energy and macros cross-checked against published Singapore kopitiam nutritional analyses. Proportions: bread 55 g per 100 g product, butter 25 g per 100 g product, sugar 20 g per 100 g product.

gi_source.title: White bread GI reference — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for white bread (GI 70, glucose reference)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singaporean butter-sugar thick toast. GI of 70 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database for white bread (glucose reference scale). Butter and sugar additions do not substantially lower the GI of the bread base in this preparation; sugar (sucrose) has GI ~65 and butter has negligible GI effect. A composite GI of 70 is defensible for this product. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (70 × 62.4) / 100 = 43.7, where carbohydrate per 120 g serving = 62.4 g.

hypertension.guidance: Each serving of butter sugar thick toast provides approximately 372 mg sodium and 13.4 g saturated fat; individuals with hypertension should limit consumption to occasional servings and monitor total daily sodium intake, as regular kopitiam toast consumption may contribute meaningfully to the recommended 2000 mg/day sodium ceiling.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI 70) and high sugar content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Butter sugar thick toast has a high GI of 70 and delivers approximately 27 g of sugar and 62 g of total carbohydrate per standard serving, producing a glycemic load of approximately 43.7; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this food and consider lower-GI bread alternatives with reduced added sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat, trans fat, and added sugar
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: A single serving of butter sugar thick toast contributes approximately 13.4 g saturated fat and 0.5 g trans fat alongside 27 g added sugar, all of which are independently associated with adverse cardiovascular outcomes; individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should limit this food to rare occasions and prioritise unsaturated fat sources.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Butter Sugar Thick Toast is a popular Singaporean kopitiam (traditional coffee shop) breakfast and snack item consisting of thick-cut white bread that is toasted until golden and crisp, then spread generously with salted butter and topped with white granulated sugar. It is a signature item at chains such as Toast Box and is closely associated with the Singaporean and Malaysian coffee-shop culture. The bread used is typically a soft, enriched white loaf sliced thicker than standard sandwich bread, giving the toast a contrast of crisp exterior and soft, pillowy interior. The butter melts into the warm toast, and the sugar provides a sweet, slightly crunchy finish. Nutritionally, this item is energy-dense at approximately 390 kcal per 100 g, with a high carbohydrate content (~52 g/100 g), significant added sugar (~22.5 g/100 g), and notable saturated fat (~11.2 g/100 g) from the butter. The glycemic index is high (GI 70), making it a high-glycemic-load food per serving. It is not suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular disease without strict portion control. It is vegetarian and halal-friendly but contains gluten and dairy allergens. Commonly served alongside kopi (local coffee) or teh (local tea) as part of a traditional Singaporean breakfast set.
common_user_queries: What is butter sugar thick toast?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Singaporean butter-sugar thick toast was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or any primary regional nutrition database. Nutrient values were estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for white toast bread, butter, and granulated sugar in proportions consistent with a typical kopitiam thick toast preparation. GI is analogously estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database white bread reference (GI 70). Confidence is medium rather than low because the component foods are well-characterised in approved sources and the proportions are consistent with published kopitiam food analyses.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data (white toast bread, butter, granulated sugar) as no direct HPB Singapore Foodo or equivalent primary regional entry exists for this specific preparation. 2. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0; this is confirmed zero as white bread, butter, and sugar contain no meaningful vitamin C — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for all three components. 3. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate based on white bread GI from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Singaporean butter-sugar thick toast was found in the peer-reviewed literature. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g (4.8), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.8), trans_fat_g (0.4), potassium_mg (95.0), calcium_mg (55.0), iron_mg (1.8), cholesterol_mg (48.0), vitamin_a_mcg_rae (145.0), vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg (0.18), vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg (0.12), vitamin_b3_niacin_mg (1.8), vitamin_b6_mg (0.05), vitamin_b12_mcg (0.08), folate_mcg_dfe (38.0), vitamin_d_mcg (0.2), vitamin_e_mg (0.6), phosphorus_mg (78.0), magnesium_mg (14.0), zinc_mg (0.6), selenium_mcg (12.0), and water_g (22.0) are all estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data and are not individually reported in a single approved source for this composite dish. 5. No -1 sentinel values remain in this record; all fields have been estimated from approved component sources. 6. Halal status assumed based on ingredients (no pork, no alcohol); formal halal certification status of specific restaurant products not verified. 7. FoodOn URI FOODON_03315091 and FoodEx2 code A00LC are used as closest parent categories; no exact ontology code exists for this specific Singaporean preparation.
